title: Neural Integration at the Surgical–Bioengineering Interface in Proprioceptive Dental Implant Prototypes
author: Das, Siddhartha
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Engineering a proprioceptive dental implant requires coupling neurogenic bioactive coatings with a surgical protocol that preserves native periodontal and neural structures.  Nanofiber coatings loaded with neurotrophic factors (NGF, BDNF, NT-3, GDNF, etc) or their small-molecule/peptide mimetics (e.g., 7,8-dihydroxyflavone, LM22A-4, Cerebrolysin, etc.) promote neural differentiation at the interface; adjunct strategies such as microRNA (miR-124, miR-9, etc) or gene-vector delivery can further enhance neurogenesis. An  apress-fit implant prototype  was developed featuring a reduced core diameter and a multilayer neurogenic nanofiber coating created through sequential fiber–bioactive deposition. Importantly,  neural integration was observed even in the absence of exogenous stem cells , demonstrating that stem-cell supplementation is not essential. The surgical protocol uses  atraumatic extraction  to preserve periodontal-ligament remnants, enabling a slightly oversized, deformable, coated implant to achieve frictional stability while supporting neural regeneration.  Together, the neurogenic coating and periodontal-preserving surgical method form an integrated strategy to drive neural integration and restore proprioceptive function in next-generation dental implants.
